title: Stiff or Stilted Gait in Cats: Understanding Mobility Issues
meta_desc: Is your cat walking stiffly? Learn about stilted gaits, why the urgency level is low, and how video triage helps your veterinarian diagnose the cause.
content_html:
TL;DR: A stiff gait in cats usually points to chronic issues like arthritis and is rarely an emergency unless accompanied by paralysis or severe distress. Providing your vet with a video of your cat moving at home is essential for an accurate diagnosis.

What does it mean if my cat has a stiff or stilted gait?

A stiff or stilted gait occurs when a cat loses its natural, fluid movement and instead walks with rigid limbs or shorter, choppy steps. You might notice your cat appearing 'wooden' or being reluctant to bend their joints while walking, jumping, or climbing stairs. This is typically a sign that the cat is experiencing discomfort, reduced range of motion, or inflammation in their joints or muscles.

Is a stiff gait in my cat considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In most instances, a stiff gait is a sign of a chronic, non-life-threatening condition such as osteoarthritis or a minor soft tissue injury.
  • While it requires a veterinary evaluation to improve your cat's quality of life, it is generally not an emergency.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if the stiffness is accompanied by a complete inability to walk, vocalizing in pain, or cold extremities.

How does providing a video of my cat help the veterinarian with triage?

  • Capture a video of your cat walking in their home environment to provide the most helpful diagnostic information.
  • Note that adrenaline at the clinic can temporarily mask stiffness, making home observation vital for an accurate assessment.
  • Video allows the veterinarian to observe the cat's natural movement in a relaxed, pain-indexed setting.
  • Providing visual evidence makes it much easier for the vet to pinpoint the source of the gait abnormality and develop an accurate treatment plan.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A stiff or stilted gait in cats, indicative of lameness, can arise from various causes, including fibrotic myopathy of the semitendinosus muscle or restrictions due to annular ligaments, adhesions, or severe fibrosis. Accurate determination of the cause is critical, as treatment varies accordingly. Some causes, such as fibrotic myopathy, produce characteristic gaits where the affected limb is pulled back and down quickly before the protraction phase, giving the impression that the foot "slaps down" on the ground, most obvious at the walk. Limb assessment should proceed from distal to proximal, with palpation of bones, joints, and soft tissues to note abnormalities such as swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, and muscle atrophy. Diagnostic imaging techniques, including radiography, ultrasonography, scintigraphy, CT, and MRI, can be helpful in identifying the underlying cause of lameness.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1096)

faqs.question: Is stiffness a normal part of a cat aging?
faqs.answer: While common in older cats, stiffness is not a 'normal' state of being; it usually indicates underlying pain from arthritis that can and should be managed with veterinary care.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relief for their stiffness?
faqs.answer: No. Human medications like Tylenol (acetaminophen) or Advil (ibuprofen) are extremely toxic to cats and can be fatal. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: What are common causes of a stilted gait?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include osteoarthritis, spinal issues, muscle strains, or nutritional deficiencies, though your vet will perform an exam to rule out more serious neurological concerns.
